mappings.rationaleAI: Core anthropological treatment of kinship systems, descent, and social organization (A, A.1, A.4), including economic exchange and property in small-scale societies (C.1, C.1.a, C.1.b, C.1.c). Covers group structures and associations (B, B.1), transmission of culture and socialization (D.1), belief systems and folklore (D.2). Connects to linguistics through its relationship with language studies and structural-functionalist analysis of cultural systems (D.5).

Legal frameworks for marriage, divorce, civil partnership, forced marriage, and sexual behavior regulation (A.2). Addresses inheritance, succession, and family as a centre for transmission of economic and legal powers and goods (A.5).
Social construction of sexual norms, reproductive rights, and the regulation of sexual behavior across cultures and historical periods (A.2).

Plural marriage as an institution across cultures, its legal and religious dimensions regarding mate selection and marriage customs (A.2), and the role of marriage in transmitting political power and nation-building (A.5).
Gender as an evolving social institution intersecting with kinship and social change (A.6). Examines class, race, and gender as systems of social stratification and status organization (B.2).

Systematic study of social institutions, group behavior, and associations within societies (B, B.1). Covers social class, stratification, and egalitarianism as organization by status (B.2). Addresses education as a social institution and formal enculturation (D.1).

History of aristocracy as a class system and organization by status from ancient origins through modern decline (B.2). Covers how aristocratic families transmitted political, economic, and social powers through inheritance and succession (A.5).
Chattel slavery as a system of social organization characterized by serfdom and forced labor, covering the legal and ideological construction of racial hierarchy (B.2).
The antislavery movement challenging systems characterized by slavery, redefining equality and social status through moral philosophy and democratic activism (B.2).

Resource allocation, market mechanisms, and division of labor as economic systems (C). Covers production and role differentiation (C.1.a), exchange of goods and distribution of wealth (C.1.b), and property rights (C.1.c).

Capitalism as an economic system centered on market exchange, capital accumulation, and trade (C). Addresses exchange of goods, distribution of wealth, and financial systems including comparative economic models (C.1.b).
Work as a social and economic phenomenon encompassing paid and unpaid labor, reflecting production and division of labor within economic systems (C, C.1.a).
Comparative analysis of economic development patterns across nations, addressing why some countries achieved prosperity while others remained poor -- a broad treatment of economic systems (C).

Education systems as formal and informal enculturation, covering how schools evolved, the tension between formal instruction and progressive methods, and education's role in socialization and democratic citizenship (D.1).
Shifting definitions and experiences of childhood as formal and informal enculturation across cultures, including the impact of reform movements on children's welfare and socialization (D.1).
Myth as a cross-cultural phenomenon encompassing religious belief, folklore, and cosmological explanation, studied through anthropology, psychology, and comparative religion (D.2).
Comparative world mythology examining myths from diverse cultures as expressions of religious belief, folklore, and transcultural themes about human origins and the nature of deity (D.2).

Ritual as a fundamental cultural expression connected to religious belief and folklore, encompassing rites of passage, sacrifice, and ceremonies that transmit beliefs and values across cultures (D.2).
Historical development of sport from folk games to modern global phenomena, covering recreation, sports, and games as cultural elements common to all societies (D.6).
Study of music across geographic and cultural diversity as an element of artistic expression common to all cultures but differing in practice between them (D.4).
Feminism as a movement challenging gender-based social organization and status systems (B.2), with marriage reform and the evolving institution of kinship and family as social change (A.6).

Confucianism as a philosophical and cultural system shaping education, socialization, and formal enculturation through civil service examinations and moral cultivation (D.1), and as a system of religious and philosophical belief underlying social relations (D.2).

Social psychology examines how individuals relate to groups and society, covering group behavior, social influence, obedience, and the varieties of associations and dynamics within societies (B, B.1).

Interdisciplinary analysis of identity formation through markers such as citizenship, gender, and cultural belonging as aspects of group association (B, B.1). Connects to linguistics through the role of language in identity formation and philosophical treatments of selfhood (D.5).
Islamic jurisprudence as a major legal system covering family law, inheritance law, and penal law across the Middle East, Asia, and Africa (D.3).
Philosophical examination of home as domestic space, covering the nature and variety of dwellings and place-attachment, directly relevant to rules of residence and dwelling patterns (A.3).

Dynastic rule as bloodline-based political legitimacy involving descent systems and lineage (A.4), the family as a centre for transmission of political power and governance through inheritance and succession (A.5), and kinship as an evolving social institution in state formation (A.6).
Theatre as a global artistic form integrating dance, literature, music, and visual arts -- a performing art as cultural expression common to societies worldwide (D.4).
Law as a foundational social institution covering criminal, civil, constitutional, and international law as legal systems operating within human societies (D.3).
